methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $25M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Nuclear Energy (found 1 same-stage, 1 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 8 same-sector peers exist in Nuclear Energy, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

private.name: Fuse Energy
private.rationale: Same sector (Nuclear Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $200M+

data.narrative:
Copenhagen Atomics is a Series A nuclear energy company that has raised $25M+ to date. The broader nuclear energy sector comprises 3 public comparables (OKLO, NNE, SMR) and 8 private peers including Fuse Energy, TerraPower, Kairos Power, X-Energy, and Oklo, though direct peer valuation data at Copenhagen Atomics' stage and scale remains sparse.

Stage-progression analysis is the primary applicable method. Using total capital raised of $25M as a valuation proxy and applying a 2.0x step-up multiplier typical of Series A to subsequent-stage progression yields an implied valuation of $50M. A peer-median approach is not supportable; while 8 same-sector comparables exist, fewer than three datapoints fall within the same stage, adjacent stage, or similar valuation tier needed for robust median benchmarking. Attempting to force a broader cross-sector median would introduce excessive noise.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $42M (low) to $57M (high), with a median point of $50M. This range reflects the single applicable triangulation method. Key uncertainties include the absence of tightly comparable private nuclear companies at identical stage and capital levels, limiting calibration of the 2.0x step-up multiplier specifically to this subsector. Outcomes will depend heavily on team execution, technology differentiation relative to named peers, and capital efficiency relative to sector norms.
